title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 9, 2026
headline: A UN inquiry finds Sudan's RSF conduct meets the legal test for genocide; Sudan's score is already at zero.
summary: A United Nations inquiry concluded on July 9 that mass killings and gang rapes committed by Sudan's Rapid Support Forces (RSF) meet the legal definition of genocide. Sudan already scores 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark, so the finding cannot push the score down further -- but it is the most serious atrocity finding this benchmark has recorded this year. Myanmar's civil war has now passed 100,000 deaths since the 2021 coup, with the military responsible for about 7 in 10 civilian deaths; Myanmar also holds at 0 of 100. Anthropic, the AI safety company, holds at 59.1 of 100 after a hidden tracking feature in its Claude Code product was discovered and removed. An Anthropic engineer confirmed the tracker was an undisclosed experiment meant to catch rival AI labs copying its models -- a real credibility problem, but a single, quickly-fixed one, so the score does not change tonight. Meta Platforms holds at 7.8 of 100, near the very bottom of the Fortune 500 list -- not 60 of 100 as an earlier internal note mistakenly suggested. A $1.4 trillion lawsuit demand from four US states over youth social-media harm is a maximum possible penalty figure, not new misconduct, so it does not move the score either. In central Africa, an Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo passed 600 deaths -- the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record -- while Uganda reported a second, separate outbreak of the related Marburg virus the same week. Venezuela's earthquake death toll rose to 3,899 as of day 16; the disaster-response score has now held at the same reduced reading for three straight nights, just under the point threshold the benchmark requires before it changes a published score.

pipeline.elSalvadorHandling: El Salvador's downgrade to 15.0 of 100 (Critical band) remains applied from 2026-07-05. El Salvador was not in tonight's priority cluster. No carry-forward action.

topSignals.title: A UN Inquiry Finds Sudan's Conduct Meets the Legal Test for Genocide; Myanmar's War Passes 100,000 Deaths
topSignals.whyItMatters: Two governments accused of the world's worst atrocities already hold the lowest possible score, so today's grim milestones cannot push either score down any further.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100, the lowest possible score. A United Nations inquiry concluded on July 9 that mass killings and gang rapes committed by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), the paramilitary group fighting Sudan's army, meet the legal definition of genocide. This is the most severe atrocity finding recorded against any country this year. It sits alongside an ongoing siege of the city of El Obeid, where roughly 500,000 people remain trapped, a situation the UN Human Rights Council is already investigating. Because Sudan's score cannot go below zero, the genocide finding is recorded as confirming the existing score rather than lowering it. Myanmar also holds at 0 of 100. New data released July 1 shows the country's civil war, which began after the military's 2021 coup, has now killed more than 100,000 people. The military itself is responsible for about 7 in 10 of the civilian deaths -- meaning most of the killing is the government's own conduct, not just fighting between armed groups. Confirmed at 0 of 100 for both Sudan and Myanmar; confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 9, 2026: a UN probe found that mass killings and gang rapes committed by Sudan's Rapid Support Forces amount to genocide.
The Irrawaddy, July 2026: Myanmar's post-coup civil war death toll surpassed 100,000, with the military responsible for the majority of civilian fatalities.

topSignals.title: Anthropic's Hidden Tracker and Meta's Corrected Score Both Hold Steady Tonight
topSignals.whyItMatters: One AI company got caught quietly tracking some of its own users; the other was mistakenly described as scoring far higher than it actually does. Neither changes the published record tonight.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100, in the Functional band, 0.9 points below the higher Established band. An independent researcher discovered that Anthropic's Claude Code product had, since roughly March 2026, secretly flagged certain users -- those whose device location matched parts of China -- by hiding a tracking signal inside normal-looking text. An Anthropic engineer confirmed the code was real and described it as an unannounced experiment meant to catch rival AI labs using Claude's output to train their own models without permission. Anthropic removed the tracker around July 1 once the discovery became public. Undisclosed user tracking is a genuine honesty problem, especially for a company that has publicly opposed government surveillance demands. But because this was a single episode that Anthropic acknowledged and fixed once caught, it does not add up to enough to move the score. Meta Platforms holds at 7.8 of 100, in the Critical band -- close to the very bottom of the Fortune 500 ranking, not 60 of 100 as an earlier internal note mistakenly described it. Court filings this week show four US states are seeking about $1.4 trillion in penalties, alleging Meta designed Facebook and Instagram to be addictive to young users. Legal experts say that figure is a maximum possible demand, not a likely outcome, and the underlying case was already the basis for Meta's low score. Confirmed at 59.1 of 100 for Anthropic and 7.8 of 100 for Meta; confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Malwarebytes, July 2026: Anthropic confirmed that a hidden tracking feature in Claude Code, active since around March 2026, flagged users in certain geographic locations and was removed after discovery, with the company describing it as an unannounced experiment.
ConsumerAffairs, July 8, 2026: four US states are seeking roughly $1.4 trillion in penalties from Meta, alleging Facebook and Instagram were deliberately designed to addict young users.

topSignals.title: Israel and Yemen Both Hold at the Lowest Possible Score as Each Government's Own Conduct Continues
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel keeps approving new West Bank settlements and Yemen's Houthi authorities keep holding UN staff in detention -- both patterns that already put each score at the floor.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. Its cabinet approved 13 new settlements in the central West Bank on July 6, a move Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich called a revolution in settlement policy. Settlers have also destroyed an electricity line, vandalized greenhouses, and attempted to seize livestock from Bedouin herders in the same window, continuing a pattern of violence UN monitors say has surged since 2023. Yemen holds at 0 of 100. The Houthi movement, which controls much of the country, continues to detain 73 United Nations and civil-society staff, some held since 2024; a World Food Programme worker previously died in Houthi custody. Both patterns are each government's own conduct, not conduct by an outside party, and both already sit at the lowest possible score, so neither can move lower. Confirmed at 0 of 100 for both; confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 6, 2026: Israel's cabinet approved 13 new West Bank settlements, which Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich described as a revolution in settlement policy.
Human Rights Watch, June 2026: the Houthi authorities in Yemen continue to detain 73 United Nations and civil society staff, some held since 2024.

topSignals.title: A Second Outbreak Hits Central Africa as Congo's Ebola Death Toll Passes 600
topSignals.whyItMatters: Congo's Ebola outbreak is now the fastest-growing on record, and Uganda reported a completely separate, related virus outbreak the same week.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds at 2.3 of 100, just above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak reached 600 confirmed deaths and 1,759 confirmed cases as of July 9 -- described by health authorities as the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak ever recorded. Only 82% of the more than 10,000 people who had contact with confirmed cases are being tracked, short of the 95% the World Health Organization says is needed to stop the spread. Ebola is caused by a virus, not by government action, so the outbreak itself is not scored against the Congo's government -- but the tracking gap is a real, if not new, sign of weak response capacity, consistent with the country's already-low score. Uganda holds at 20.3 of 100, in the Developing band, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Uganda reported a completely separate outbreak of Marburg virus, a relative of Ebola, on June 30, even as its own Ebola cluster has shown no new case in more than two weeks. Uganda's prompt report to the World Health Organization is treated as a small positive sign about its own response, not a mark against it. Confirmed at 2.3 of 100 for the Democratic Republic of the Congo and 20.3 of 100 for Uganda; confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 9, 2026: confirmed Ebola deaths in the Democratic Republic of the Congo reached 600, in what health officials describe as the fastest-growing outbreak on record.
STAT News, June 30, 2026: Uganda reported a new Marburg virus outbreak in the west of the country, separate from its ongoing Ebola response.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Death Toll Climbs to 3,899 as Five Countries' Scores Hold Under Watch
topSignals.whyItMatters: Venezuela's earthquake-response score keeps falling just short of the point threshold that would trigger a public score change, for the third night in a row.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18 of 100. Its earthquake death toll rose to 3,899 as of day 16 (July 9), up from 3,535 two days earlier, now the deadliest natural disaster in the country in more than a century; US Geological Survey modeling suggests the true final toll could exceed 10,000. Recalculating Venezuela's disaster-response performance for this evidence would put the score at 15.3, a decline of 2.7 points -- the same reduced reading confirmed on July 7 and July 8, still short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before changing a published score. Four other countries hold at their published scores tonight while carrying a documented downward-trending watch: Nigeria (18 of 100), where 17 million or more people face crisis-level hunger across the north; China (19.5 of 100), where a new ethnic-assimilation law continues to be enforced; Pakistan (17.2 of 100), where more bodies were recovered from a Balochistan region enforced-disappearance pattern showing signs of torture; and Lebanon (17.2 of 100), where the main source of harm remains outside fighting rather than the Lebanese government's own conduct. None of these five crosses the threshold for a score change tonight. Confirmed at 18 of 100 for Venezuela and Nigeria, 19.5 of 100 for China, and 17.2 of 100 for Pakistan and Lebanon; confidence: high for all five.

evidence.claim: WMNF, July 9, 2026: Venezuela's earthquake death toll rose to 3,899 as of day 16, with 16,740 injured and 17,907 displaced.
Daily Post, July 9, 2026: the World Food Programme warns that food assistance cuts are imminent across northern Nigeria as hunger deepens faster than projected.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; fourth cycle since the July 5 downgrade was applied.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. No confirmed enforcement action abroad this cycle; no repeat discrete triggering event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. A hidden tracking feature in its Claude Code product was discovered and removed this week; the episode is real but sub-threshold because it was a single, acknowledged, corrected incident.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. The labor board review has not yet concluded. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. The main source of harm within the window remains the Israel-Hezbollah conflict, not Lebanon's own conduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. More bodies were recovered from Balochistan showing signs of torture this cycle, reinforcing rather than deepening the existing pattern.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. Day 16 earthquake response evidence would move the score to 15.3 if applied -- a 2.7-point decline, unchanged for a third consecutive check and still under the 5-point threshold for a change.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. The World Food Programme now describes northern hunger as the worst in nearly a decade, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak has now passed 600 deaths, the fastest-growing on record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: US-Iran talks resume July 11 in Doha. A documented lethal crackdown on political rivals would move Iran's score toward the floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight; the state funeral for Ayatollah Khamenei concluded July 9 with burial in Mashhad.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor, in its twenty-fifth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight; sixteenth floor-proximity cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight; eleventh boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. The calibration question against peer country Burkina Faso has been open 16 consecutive days and is now treated as a standing item for the benchmark's own coordinating team, not a routine nightly note.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Mali remains open 16 consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight; neither legal matter has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10 -- one day away -- but that is US conduct, not scored against Haiti.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100 and enters the watch list for the first time, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. A new Marburg outbreak was reported this cycle alongside an already-active Ebola response; Uganda's prompt WHO notification is treated as a small positive sign, not a mark against it.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list -- not 60 of 100 as an earlier internal note mistakenly described it. A $1.4 trillion penalty demand disclosed this week is a litigation maximum figure already reflected in the existing score, not new conduct.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's genocide finding and Myanmar's 100,000-death milestone both document worsening severity that the lowest possible score cannot reflect with a further point drop.
emergingRisks.detail: Both countries already hold 0 of 100, the lowest possible score. The benchmark records each new atrocity finding as confirming, not lowering, that score, since there is no lower number available.

emergingRisks.risk: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the higher Established band after a hidden tracking feature in Claude Code was discovered and removed.
emergingRisks.detail: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the tracker was broader or kept running longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each be enough to move the score toward a change.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring question has now run 16 consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's coordinating team.
emergingRisks.detail: The question is whether Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100), two military governments with comparably documented failures to protect civilians, are being scored consistently against each other. It is now the longest-open question the benchmark is tracking.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has held at a 2.7-point sub-threshold decline for three consecutive checks with no independent confirmation of the undercount allegation.
emergingRisks.detail: Independent verification of a forensic pathologist's claim that the true death toll is far higher than reported would be the event that moves this from a documented watch to a scored change.

emergingRisks.risk: Central Africa now has two active hemorrhagic-fever outbreaks at once: Congo's Ebola outbreak past 600 deaths and Uganda's separate Marburg outbreak.
emergingRisks.detail: There is no approved vaccine for this Ebola strain. The outbreak's natural seasonal peak runs through July 31. Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger toward the absolute floor for either country.

emergingRisks.risk: Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10.
emergingRisks.detail: This is United States conduct, not Haitian conduct, and continues to be tracked as a forward trigger for the US score even though neither country was fully in tonight's assessed cluster on this point.

emergingRisks.risk: Eleven of the 15 countries and companies assessed tonight carry a documented downward-trending watch -- the highest share recorded so far in July.
emergingRisks.detail: None of these individually crosses the threshold for a score change, but the concentration is worth tracking as its own trend line distinct from the count of formal proposals, which has stayed at zero for four consecutive nights.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sudan and Myanmar -- Gravest Atrocity Findings of the Series, Both Floor-Confirmed
sectorAlerts.headline: A UN inquiry finds Sudan's RSF conduct meets the legal test for genocide while Myanmar's civil war passes 100,000 deaths; both countries hold at 0 of 100.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): the United Nations concluded July 9 that mass killings and gang rapes by the Rapid Support Forces meet the legal definition of genocide, the most severe atrocity finding recorded this year.
Myanmar (0 of 100): the post-coup civil war has now killed more than 100,000 people, with the military responsible for about 7 in 10 civilian deaths.
Both countries already hold the lowest possible score, so neither finding can push the score down further -- it is recorded as confirming, not lowering, the existing score.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs and Fortune 500 -- Anthropic and Meta Both Hold Steady After Special Review
sectorAlerts.headline: Anthropic's hidden Claude Code tracker and Meta's corrected 7.8-of-100 score both confirm without a change tonight.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100): a hidden tracking feature in Claude Code, active since roughly March 2026, was discovered and removed; an Anthropic engineer confirmed it was an undisclosed experiment to detect misuse by rival AI labs.

Meta Platforms (7.8 of 100): near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list, not 60 of 100 as an earlier internal note mistakenly described it. A $1.4 trillion penalty demand from four US states is a litigation maximum figure, already reflected in the score.
Both hold at their published scores tonight with a documented downward-trending watch carried forward.

sectorAlerts.sector: Central Africa -- Two Hemorrhagic-Fever Outbreaks at Once
sectorAlerts.headline: Congo's Ebola outbreak passes 600 deaths -- the fastest-growing on record -- as Uganda reports a separate Marburg outbreak the same week.
observations: Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3 of 100): 600 confirmed Ebola deaths and 1,759 cases as of July 9; contact-tracing coverage sits at 82%, short of the 95% the World Health Organization says is needed.
Uganda (20.3 of 100): reported a new Marburg virus outbreak June 30, even as its own Ebola cluster shows no new case in more than two weeks.
Neither outbreak is scored as government-caused harm; the scoreable element is each government's own response, which stays within its existing range for both countries.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America -- Venezuela's Earthquake Response Holds for a Third Consecutive Check
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake death toll climbs to 3,899 as the disaster-response score holds at the same sub-threshold reading for a third straight cycle.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the death toll rose to 3,899 as of day 16, now the deadliest natural disaster in the country in more than a century.
Recalculating for this evidence would move the score to 15.3, a 2.7-point decline -- unchanged from the July 7 and July 8 readings and still under the 5-point threshold for a change.
This remains the leading candidate for a formal score change if the undercount allegation is independently verified.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Question Reaches 16 Days
sectorAlerts.headline: The open question of whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct is now the longest-running item the benchmark is tracking.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100): a militant offensive resumed July 4 after a two-month lull, reinforcing existing deficits without a new discrete escalation.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Mali remains open.
The question has now run 16 consecutive days without a decision, and is treated as a standing item for the benchmark's coordinating team rather than a routine nightly note.

sectorAlerts.sector: Middle East and South Asia -- Continuation of Already-Priced Patterns
sectorAlerts.headline: Israel and Yemen hold at the floor on continuing own-conduct, while Pakistan, Lebanon, China, and Nigeria hold on already-documented patterns.
observations: Israel (0 of 100): the cabinet approved 13 new West Bank settlements July 6 alongside continuing settler violence.
Yemen (0 of 100): Houthi authorities continue detaining 73 UN and civil-society staff.
Pakistan (17.2), Lebanon (17.2), China (19.5), and Nigeria (18.0) all hold at published scores tonight, each carrying a documented downward-trending watch.

methodologyNotes.name: A Corrected Baseline: Meta Platforms Scores 7.8, Not 60
methodologyNotes.description: An earlier internal note described Meta Platforms as scoring 60 of 100 at the boundary between two upper bands. That was wrong. Meta's actual published score, checked directly against the live public record, is 7.8 of 100 -- near the very bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Tonight's assessment used the correct 7.8 figure. The benchmark's rule is simple: the live, published score is always the authoritative one, and any internal note that disagrees with it gets corrected before publication.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score That Is Already at Zero Can Still Record a Worse Finding
methodologyNotes.description: Sudan and Myanmar both score 0 of 100, the lowest possible mark. Tonight's news -- a UN genocide finding for Sudan, and Myanmar's civil war passing 100,000 deaths -- cannot lower either score further, because there is no lower number to give. The benchmark records these findings as confirming the existing floor score rather than as a new downgrade, so the public record shows the evidence without implying a score change that cannot happen.

methodologyNotes.name: Two Data-Tracking Corrections Fixed Before Assessment
methodologyNotes.description: Uganda's internal working score had drifted to 15.3 in the Critical band, but the correct, published score has been 20.3 in the Developing band all along. Myanmar's internal rank had drifted to 191 instead of the correct 186. Both were caught and corrected before tonight's assessment began, following the same rule used for the Meta Platforms correction: the live, published score is always the authoritative one.

methodologyNotes.name: Eleven of Fifteen Assessed Entities Carry a Downward-Trending Watch Tonight -- a New High for July
methodologyNotes.description: A country or company can hold at its published score while still carrying a documented downward-trending watch -- a signal that real, negative evidence exists but has not yet reached the size needed to change the published number. Tonight, 11 of the 15 entities assessed carry this kind of watch, up from 10 on July 8 and 4 on July 7, even though zero score-change proposals were generated for a fourth consecutive night. The benchmark tracks these two figures separately because they measure different things.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Temporary Protected Status work authorization expires for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States. This is United States conduct, not scored against Haiti.

United States-Iran talks resume in Doha following the close of the state funeral window. A verifiable agreement would be a positive scored event; a breakdown combined with new provocation would be a negative one.

The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Sixteen days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
First confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
An implemented ceasefire with unobstructed humanitarian access at El Obeid is the only path to a positive scored event from the absolute floor.
Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Established boundary after a hidden Claude Code tracker was confirmed and removed; would a second undisclosed telemetry episode, or a regulator finding of deceptive practice, move the Integrity dimension enough to cross that boundary before the EU AI Act's August 2 enforcement date?
